OE1: While basic needs have not impeded my ability to work, I see it regularly interfering with students' academic pursuits. Our campus is not within walking distance of many neighborhoods, we do not provide any kind of on-campus housing, there is limited public transportation available, and students often struggle with getting enough food and getting nutrient-dense, healthful food that doesn't lead to later health problems such as diabetes and heart disease. These issues are only multiplied when students have children or other dependents to care for, making it very difficult for them to access education as a pathway to a sustainable wage.
OE2: I am really proud of our campus' recent Food Security Initiative in launching the Campus Cabinet food bank for current and prospective students. I would like to see the Campus Cabinet grow to increase capacity with food pick ups and include basic hygiene and household items such as shampoo, soap, deodorant, detergents, etc. I would also like to see our campus address our lack of on-campus housing. Since we are a community college in a small city serving primarily non-traditional students, I don't know that dorms would be feasible, but I would like to see us partner with local housing programs to provide subsidized housing for students or perhaps launch a roommate-finding program to help students reduce housing costs.
OE3: Thank you for doing this survey!

OE1: I am very resentful to my employer for not paying me better. In Florida I made over double what I am paid here. OE2: They could pay us a living wage. After taxes, I make about 12 dollars an hour... again, my position requires years of experience and a Masters degree.
OE3: Clovis Community College is a dump. They borderline steal from student with their ridiculous text-book rental scheme, gouge the fees so that the price here is nearly as high as ENMU, and do not drop students for nonpayment, despite telling them that they do, leading to students not knowing they are still enrolled in their courses, so they fail them. I do not know how we keep our accreditation and I can't wait to quit this job. It will take me a decade to financially and professionally recover from the two years I have spent here. Luckily, my husband has orders out-of-state, so this nightmare is almost over. I do not know how my co-workers, most of whom are hourly workers survive.

Income_other: It depends on how many hours I am allowed to work per 4-8 week session, break days, summers (some are on some are off), If I am granted overtime.
OE1: Preoccupies my brain and emotional power. I drag myself to work, have a hard time connecting with people and having patience since some days these insecurities are greater, I have a lower productivity level.
OE2:
Be more open to diversity when hiring and paying employees and giving that extension to students who may become employees. I have applied to internal jobs for which I am qualified (or very slightly under qualified). There is a clear bias (gender, personality, ability to manipulate) in who is hired. 
Diversity when paying, I have more qualifications, experience, and oversee more projects than anyone however the degree is what determines the pay (I cannot afford to pursue a new degree even if it benefits the college). I can get work dones (when my mind is clear) 2-4 times faster, yet get payed per hour at the same rate. My tasks are complete and I get payed for 1 hour when someone else gets paid for 3 just cause it took them longer. 25% of my pay goes to taxes and more if I had insurance through them. Take home pay is 12 dollars and 13 dollars for many full time workers. 
I do not want free housing (I like my house I just need to afford it). We do not free food ( I see too much free food get thrown away. We need reasonable pay for living and buying what we need. Strategies: I haven't done any of these
Experience: Although I currently am able to handle all of my basic needs in partnership with my husband, in my past I was homeless three times; all of these instances were times of transition while I was single, in college and moving in and out of different types of housing. I handled the issue by making extensive use of the school's library to study, sleeping on friend's couches or floors, borrowing money until I could find employment, and accepting meals from people when they offered to pay. If I'd had a car, I might have slept in it, but I did not own a car at that time. (Incidentally, I am grateful to every single person who helped me out during that time, and most have remained my life-long friends).
Trans_pub_notuse: There is no public transportation near my home
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc)
OE1: I am currently not experiencing housing security. Food security is only a supply-chain issue for me at present. Consequently, neither is affecting my work at present.
OE2: Although I do not see it as the college administrator's fault, our campus really needs to identify and secure a reliable food vendor to operate our snack bar which offers accessibly priced, decent food. Perhaps partnering with a local fast-food restaurant to operate a small-scale satellite location of their larger franchise in our on-campus snack bar location might work. I realize McDonald's is not the highest quality food, but it has menu options that are nutritious, and reasonably priced. And, of course, it is popular!
OE3: Although I have reached a place wherein I am secure of my housing and food, it was not always that way in my life. I realize I am fortunate in that working hard, and being frugal paid off and that good habits often do not pay off for everyone in a similar way. I have not forgotten how it felt to be homeless, have no money, and be truly insecure of my basic needs being met. I am not sure anyone could truly "get over it."

Experience:
I'm an unmarried, child-free professional (university staff) in my 40s, so all of my answers to this point of the survey reflect the financial comfort that comes with my demographic situation.  

However, during college ~20 years ago, I scraped by to pay my own rent, grocery bill, utilities, etc.  I lived on cheap carbs (mac n cheese, rice, etc.). The university needs to receive funding specifically dedicated to supporting student basic needs to support basic needs staff and services for students. The pantry on campus should be open more than 2 days for 3 hrs each day to be most accessible to the greatest number of students in need. Experience: I just recently got this job with UNM and still have my previous job as a server. Before, I would pick up extra shifts because I needed money to pay rent, get gas, or buy groceries and I would be struggling to work so much and complete assignments. I would always get the full amount on my student loans so that I could use that to pay down my credit card a little more because the payments are so crippling sometimes and keep the extra for more basic necessities. I could not afford to pay for the parking pass at UNM and would park a mile away and bike to classes, and hate the extra money needed for gas when the semester started.
Trans_pub_notuse_why: I have a car so it is not needed.
Disability: Living with a physical disability,Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc)
OE1: I know that I do not have a lot of extra money to spend on food, so I try to buy just what I need and have foods that go a long way and do not cost as much. This does not effect my work very much, but I do have an extra job in addition to my full time job which I use for groceries throughout the week to lessen my stress over affording food. I have less free time and sometimes go to bed much later than I would want because I get stuck at my other job.
OE2: Healthier options would be amazing. Having it also be affordable, because healthier food options always seem to be much more expensive. If UNM worked with the state to see if more of their students could easily qualify for food stamps because school takes time away from work (and classes are often during peak work days and hours). Or even make the SNAP benefits at the state level easier for all college students. I tried for a couple of years to get food stamps and never qualified, yet affording food for myself was so stressful. Being single makes it much harder to qualify than it would be for a single parent or family, yet that population sometimes needs help, too. In our country a college education is such a privilege because there are so many obstacles.
OE3: I have a good support system and am lucky enough to receive Medicaid, but if I did not have those I cannot imagine where I would be. Many people attending universities have a lot less. UNM has some programs to help their students, but they are still very expensive which is by far the biggest obstacle for students. I attended college there in my mid 20s so I had no scholarship even though I was a great student with great grades. I now have student loans I will be paying off for the next few decades. Making college classes less expensive, having cheaper parking passes, and better scholarships would go a long way to making housing and food more secure. SO much money went into my education and even more with interest will, too. Books, supplies, transportation, less working hours, and time away from home all cost money, too.

Experience:
I recently found out that my rent is going up by over $150 a month unless I move. I was distraught because I love the place I live right now, and I have no savings for a deposit on a new place. I borrowed over $500 from a close friend (who has the money because he lost his place to live, and his parents aren't charging him rent), and about $200 from my parents so I could pay the deposit, fees, and first month's rent on a tiny apartment in an unsafe part of town. 
This move, along with working full time and taking 9 credit hours a semester is stressing me out. I worry that rent will just keep going up and that I will have to find a new job that pays more and drop out of school just to afford my necessities.  In addition, I'm having to sell some of my furniture and valuables, which is eating into my time to focus on class. I often skip my evening class because I have to pack. I took two full days off of work to move (thank goodness UNM is so generous will annual leave). 
If I'm, as lucky and privileged as I am, experiencing these things, how is someone much less fortunate than myself getting by? How are people who can't borrow from friends and family doing this?

Foodpantry_reasons: Other students need this help more than I do
Foodavail: I put 0 to 5 dollars as the cost of a nutritious meal on campus, but the reality is that there is no such thing as a nutritious meal option on our campus.  We currently do not have a cafeteria on campus, so the only food options are snacks from vending machines and the bookstore.  These snacks just small snacks and not meals.  They are also highly processed foods, so if anyone wants to eat something nutritious, they must either bring it from home or eat off campus.  When we did have a small cafeteria on campus, there were not as many cheaper options on the menu.  Prices were not too high, but there was not a variety of differently priced options for whole meals that were not snacks or sides.  There were also not very many nutritious options or options for people with dietary restrictions.  They were also not consistent with their hours, so sometimes people would come in that morning, but would not be able to have breakfast because it was closed.  This happened to me once before as well.  That being said, that food vendor is no longer on campus.  However, we have just started up a food pantry, so students will at least have some more options of obtaining food.  This is a good thing and I am happy that this change was implemented and it bodes well for our future.  We started a community garden as well, so as that grows there should not only be more food availability on campus, but also more nutritious options.  Overall, I think we are improving a little in the area of food availability.

OE1: Currently, my work is not affected by food or housing insecurity on a personal level.  Since I work on a college campus, I affected by others having food or housing insecurity by trying to understand ways concerning how to best help them.
OE2: Right now we are doing a good job starting up the food pantry for students, and working on a community garden.  I know that some of my co-workers either work or have had to work multiple jobs in order to live sustainably, so having more conversations about that and coming up with solutions would be good.  While having good food security at home is the best option, I think having a cafeteria with consistent hours and reasonable food prices could potentially be helpful for our student and staff population.  As for housing, we don't offer anything that I am aware of to address that issue.  We can only point others to outside resources that are available.

OE1: I have relocated in order to find a job that pays enough for the cost of living in my area.
OE2:
Give raises, hire staff that have commensurate skills for the job, pay people what their skills are worth in the competitive market.  

The State Auditor should do a salary evaluation for all State Universities and make the salaries and wages consistent.  NMT employees are paid 20%-25% lower than their counterparts at UNM and NMSU.

Housing in Socorro is sub par in many cases and controlled by a small group of people who conspire to set rent prices for their tenants according to their salaries which are made public.  The University should consider that rental housing is both expensive and limited when determining employment offers; or, alternatively, work with local realtors to assist in setting fair prices for students and employees.

Public utilities in Socorro are extremely expensive with no visibility as to how rates are determined.  As NMT is the largest customer in the city, it has the ability to question the rates and ample staff to pursue answers to questions on rates. 

Many NMT employees attain high levels of management with high salary levels due to seniority or nepotism and do not have the requisite skills or education for their positions.  This is a high risk for the university and prevents qualified people from accessing these positions in the long term.

I Having been a student in the bad old days some of the issues with food was that I did not qualify for any assistance,  My undergraduate was from Texas A&M and they had a great program from former students that supplied loans that did not have interest to pay tuition which could be paid off at the end of semester.  There were enough jobs around that I could work several and pay it off.  Graduated without debt.  Here there are not enough jobs even though the city is much bigger and federal loans is the only way, especially for graduate students, to make ends meet.  The grad students at Texas had health insurance which eased my burden.  Of course, the state had approved such a thing.  

One of my biggest peeves was from last years ice storm.  Students were stranded on campus and UNM did not do anything.  Calling school closed late and no preparations was very dangerous.  I was busing students to my daughters trailer since she lives on south campus.  CIty buses were stranded and I only found out later that the sun van has 4-wheel drive.  Still there were students that were stuck at the library and had no way home and the library was closing.  I would love to see a plan if students are trapped where they can shelter until the danger passes and they can get off campus.

OE2: Food and Housing insecurity is a wide spread issue and has a certain class stigma attached to it. The best way to overcome stigmas is to talk about the issue, not about who or why. I believe our places of public gatherings should offer space for the community to provide for themselves. These places that were built for the community should have community gardens and instead of decorative vegetation, food producing trees and plants should be in abundance. In the harvest season, the community should be able to use a shared space to practice food preservation. In the off season (winter) there should be green houses or high tunnels where winter crops could be grown. Extra food should be given to the food pantries. It is crazy to me that 50% of the food is thrown in the trash while others do not know where their next bite will come from.

Obstacles: Because we live in a rural and depressed area that is at least an hour away from from the next larger town/city in the state, we suffer from not having the choice of better healthier food options because the local grocery stores are limited by their companies to the 2nds - if we are lucky, 3rds and 4ths regularly. It generally costs more than at the stores in the larger cities where consumers have a choice as to where they shop. We don't and the stores' management knows it. Experience:
I do not know if my experience matches housing insecurity, because I am sheltered with my parents. But, I am preparing to move out. I have a stable, secure job and 43k yearly salary before taxes, but the number of places that match what I could put to rent is almost non-existent in Santa Fe and small in Albuquerque and surrounding areas. I make too much to apply to income-restricted apartments, but too little to live in much beyond a studio. I do not want to partner up with a roommate, either. I SHOULD be able to live alone, and have a bedroom. These conditions pushed me to look out of the state. 

This is my motivation for finishing an M.S., to get more, but in looking for places to live and finding none, I have certainly been given over to anxiety and bouts of sadness, and of course it's taken time from being able to read for class or want to sit down and do homework rather than play games or watch something that helps me escape. It's caused fights, which also distract from work. I don't want to be stuck here--but how is it that I can have a respectable job that pays decently, if not competitively, and not be able to stand on my own two feet?
Disability: Living with a mental health condition
Gender2: Other or prefer not to say
OE1: It is a distraction and it depresses motivation. Thinking about what I am going to have to do once I am done with school lingers on my mind a lot, which sucks focus. But, also, the feeling that I might have to leave to be able to make enough to have a space makes me question why I work as hard as I do. It also has the effect of impacting my loyalty, not just my work ethic, but how positively I regard my workplace.
OE2: The simplest implementation, though a large demand, would be to increase wages.
OE3: I see opportunity in our numerous empty buildings to create shelter, that is managed. We have the space, we just need the will.

OE1: I am very concerned about students and trainees impacted by housing and food insecurity, particular medical students and residents. There are fewer supports in place for professional students than undergraduates, their schedules are extremely intense, they are likely to be in high debt situations, their work hours are extremely long so many services would be closed when the students would be able to access them, and there is great stigma about reaching out for help.
OE2: Graduate and professional students should be intentional targets for support and awareness campaigns. A food pantry site on north campus with long hours (or an open fridge/pantry that could be accessed at any time), grant money to help trainees with temporary housing, dedicated social workers and basic needs supporters for north campus to help manage the case loads. I believe once the needs are really investigated, graduate and professional students will be a very high needs population.
OE3: The intersection of housing/food insecurity and intimate partner violence is very strong. People who are leaving abusive relationships may have high, accute, time sensitive needs, and we need interim as well as long term supports for them, and the current supports through the Women's Resource Center are wonderful but extremely understaffed and underfunded. They cannot support all of the need that exists.

Our cafeteria is still closed. The coffee shop operated by a private vendor with some burritos has closed.  Many times no food is available on campus. The college is not in walking distance to places where you could purchase food. So you have to bring food from home. If you get hungry after 2 p.m. then usually nothing is available to purchase on campus.

OE1: Right now I am not impacted. I can pay our mortgage, but I worry about losing my job in a recession.  I also realize funds are limited for home repairs, etc. Also, I am concerned about the issues that particularly SFCC students and adjunct faculty are facing. It's obvious the reason that some of our students have left the college is because they had to leave Santa Fe and cannot afford to live here. They often move to Albuquerque or further away. Sometimes they can handle the commute, but it interferes with their ability to even have a part-time job. As gas prices rise, they will not want to commute great distances to finish their degrees.
OE2: Offer regular food service with discounted (affordable)and/or free meals. Loss of cental food services have left many feeling isolated and students leave campus as soon a class is over, since there is no longer a place to visit with friends and eat on a regular basis.  State would need to address these solutions: full coverage of tuition, fees and books to free up funding for living expenses. Housing needs to be addressed by the city and state. While dorms are not usually considered for community colleges, I think if SFCC could offer limited dormitories for students who are required to spend large amounts of time in person on campus such as nursing students. I also think it would be worth exploring if the nearby IAIA campus has any empty dorm rooms that could be made available to SFCC students, but we'd need to find funds to help students with those expenses. It would also be helpful if we had more emergency funds available to students who need to move, etc. We do offer some limited funds via the SFCC Foundation, but I don't think they are authorized to give the amounts that would be needed to pay first and last month's deposit before someone is evicted. Also, if the state could assist with offering covered or more affordable health insurance for employees it would free up money for food and housing expenses. In general, rental options are very expensive and limited. Santa Fe seniors are on the waiting list for housing assistance for years. Young people do not have access to any inexpensive housing options. Santa Fe is slowly becoming a community where the poor and middle class have no where to live.
OE3: I hope the state legislature will prioritize higher education as a way for students to gain access to better paying jobs as well as become more rounded and informed citizens. I'm glad elementary education and high school education are being more supported. But individuals working in higher education in New Mexico do not feel valued. I am concerned since I've seen staff and faculty leave the college to go work in the elementary and high schools since they can earn more pay there. They have more steps to earn higher wages, etc. in the lower-level schools. Our college needs to retain quality staff, adjunct and full-time faculty to meet the challenges in our community.

Experience: The stress of worrying about how I am going to buy food and pay the bills impacts my work performance.  There is an ongoing fear about how I am going to get through this, how am I going to make ends meet?  I often am in meetings talking about these issues in our community and it is difficult because it is an emotional topic for me and I don't know if I should talk about my struggles or not. In one meeting, where I did comment on my financial struggles, a person in a leadership role told me that I need to find a husband. Everyone laughed, but it wasn't funny for me. I turned in my parking pass b/c I cannot afford to pay for it anymore.  Also relevant: I need medical care on an ongoing basis, and I can't afford my treatments even though I have this job with health insurance.   Spending more money on healthcare = less money for food and bills. This impacts my work b/c I have higher pain levels when I cannot get treatment and end up using more sick leave as a result.
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc),Living with a mental health condition
Race2: Other or prefer not to say
OE1: I am not able to put forth my best self and my best work when I am hungry and stressing about how to pay the bills.  We are judged on how we "show up" and it's hard when your clothes are old, you need a haircut, you can't contribute to an office gift or potluck, can't afford the healthcare that you need, etc. I know that I could be doing more in my work if I wasn't carrying the weight of this stress with me all the time and I had the money that I need to take care of myself.
OE2:
Better pay - equitable employment practices. Pay people for the work they are doing. Example: My responsibilities are manager level but my title and pay are not. 

Let people decide how much money to contribute to retirement.
OE3: Thank you for asking these questions. Many of us are stuck btw. not qualifying for assistance and not being able to pay the bills.  We need more services on a sliding scale. When someone does not qualify for assistance b/c they're $20 over the income limit, that $20 is not enough to pay for any services.

Experience: This experience has not been in the past 12 months, but when I was first divorced, I had to start all over and mortgage a new home for myself and my children.  I lost my job and almost lost my home.  I found part-time work until I finally found a full-time job almost a year later.  During that year that I was jobless, I quit paying all my bills except the electricity, gas and groceries because that was all I could afford and that is what we absolutely needed to survive. My house was about to be forclosed on, but thankfully for the pandemic (ironically), the banks had many programs to help homeowners.  Thankfully, today I am blessed to say that I have a great job and I am able to pay all of my bills.  That year really humbled me and nearly broke my spirit.  I learned a lot, especially to never take anything for granted and to never lose your faith.

Experience: When I was an undergraduate student, I experienced food and housing insecurity that had a direct impact on my ability to go to class or work, focus, study, or complete assignments. I found myself facing a difficult choice between paying for rent and tuition or buying food for myself. I eventually decided to forego paying for rent and instead used the money to purchase enough food to get me through the month. Although this meant that I would have to leave my apartment and stay with friends, I felt that it was necessary to ensure that I had enough to eat. This decision ended up having a major impact on my productivity and overall wellbeing. Without access to my own kitchen, I was unable to cook meals for myself and instead had to rely on takeout or pre-packaged foods. This ultimately made it difficult to concentrate in class or stay motivated to finish my assignments. Additionally, the lack of a consistent living space caused me to feel constantly anxious and overwhelmed. In the end, I was able to manage the hardship and eventually regain stability, but the experience challenged me both academically and emotionally in ways that I had never expected.

Experience: Because my housing situation is unusual, I will describe it. I currently live in a small travel trailer (about 17 ft long) with my husband on a piece of land we purchased about 50 miles south of Albuquerque, where I work. We chose to do this because renting in the city is very expensive and also unstable - our last landlord tried to evict us for reasons he made up, thinking we would be intimidated and leave. We used the free legal resources for renters available in NM and fought back in order to stay for the rest of the rental agreement. That experience was so stressful that we decided never to rent again. We didn't have enough money for a mortgage on a house, so we bought a piece of land with a well and an electric pole and decided to live in our RV, which is where we are now. I commute 2 hours every day to Albuquerque and back, which is a lot, but at least I am employed. I can afford to buy groceries and gas, and I have health insurance. While I was unemployed, we were technically homeless - living in our RV and moving around from one NM state park to another every 2 weeks, which is much cheaper than renting a space in an RV park, if you 'dry camp'. That means no hookups - no electricity and no running water. So we put out a solar panel and hauled water. I am sharing all this because many people are not aware of this kind of housing insecurity. There are a lot of people who live this way, especially in NM. Also, I just want to say that the money I make as full-time staff is not enough to afford housing of any kind in Albuquerque, unless I spent more than half my paycheck, which is unrealistic. I found a creative housing solution that works for me, but so many people can't, even with full-time jobs.
Trans_pub_notuse_why: I would use the RailRunner if UNM provided more options to and from campus from the downtown Abq stop.
Disability: Living with a mental health condition
OE1: Housing insecurity is no longer an issue for me, but the compromise I reached of living far from the city takes a toll in terms of my time (and gas money).
OE2: Rental subsidies or stipends for students, staff, faculty
OE3: Many full-time UNM staff do not earn a true living wage for Albuquerque in 2023. The pay structure needs to take the crazy inflation of the past few years into account.
